children.label: There is at least one persona with a viable nurturing business
Small business owners at organizations under 20 who also do selling are a concrete initial persona for nurturing
There is a recurring nurturing problem worth solving
There is at least one concrete reason this persona needs better nurturing
The persona feels it is losing opportunities because of poor nurturing
children.test: Mom test: ask for concrete lost-opportunity examples where weak follow-up or weak top-of-mind presence mattered

children.label: Helping users stay top of mind is a viable nurturing angle
Users can describe concrete top-of-mind pain and missed opportunities
children.test: Mom test: ask for concrete examples where weak follow-up or weak top-of-mind presence caused a missed opportunity
children.reason: Out of 41 that answered, 33 described top-of-mind behavior. Only 1 rejected directly.

children.label: Users react strongly to a mockup that helps them stay top of mind with important contacts
children.test: Mockup: show a stay-top-of-mind workflow and measure whether users want it for real contacts now

children.label: Users are willing to pay for a top-of-mind solution if it proves value
children.test: Pricing / commitment test tied specifically to the stay-top-of-mind value proposition

children.label: Current alternatives for nurturing are weak, inconsistent, or too effortful
Users have tried and abandoned multiple nurturing channels and feel they may be missing better ways
children.test: Mom test across transcripts/interviews: channels tried and stopped, and evidence of uncertainty or FOMO

children.label: Even when users pick a nurturing channel, they do not know what to write, which medium to use, how often to reach out, or whether it works
children.test: Mom test: evidence of uncertainty about execution, abandoned attempts, or lack of a clear playbook

children.label: There is at least one concrete behavioral pattern that points to a real nurturing need
Users already try to deepen communication channels with important contacts, and may adopt help doing it
Users actively try to increase communication channels with important contacts during existing conversations
children.test: Mom test: when do users try to move a relationship to a deeper channel, and what examples can they recall?

children.label: Users prefer AI-drafted messages with natural channel upgrade versus plain versions and activate the feature
children.test: Mockup: show regular vs channel-deepening drafts and measure preference plus willingness to use

children.label: Users manually research who to meet when traveling or track when contacts are visiting their city
children.test: Mom test: ask for recent travel examples and whether they searched for people to meet or noticed visitors manually

children.label: Users will adopt a low-friction workflow to tell Gordy who to nurture and act on prompts
Users can define workable filtering criteria for who they want to nurture
Users are willing to commit to a specific list of people to nurture
children.test: Mockup: when shown contacts matching their criteria, do they select specific people rather than deferring or saying 'everyone'?

children.label: Users respond to automated drift alerts and ask for help re-engaging a real contact
children.test: MVT: send drift alerts when a user has not spoken to a contact in 3+ months and measure reply / action rate

children.label: Users engage with Gordy when he offers networking strategy advice by email
children.test: MVT concierge: Gordy emails users offering strategic networking advice. Measure reply rate and engagement depth.

children.label: There is at least one credible growth path for the nurturing product
Contact-based nudges can drive both usage and acquisition
Users naturally think of their contacts in circles or clusters rather than only as one-off relationships
children.test: Interview: ask users how they mentally organize important contacts and whether circles/clusters are already a natural model

children.label: Users will send a suggested nudge message when shown a concrete example
children.test: Mockup: show a suggested nudge on a real contact and measure send intent / actual send rate

children.label: Users who say yes to the nudge want to try the service immediately
children.test: During interviews, after a positive nudge reaction, ask whether they want to try the service now

children.label: Cold outbound offering these nurturing tips can get enough response to be a viable acquisition path
children.test: Outbound campaign: email describing the value of concrete nurturing tips. Measure yes rate and response rate.

children.label: Recipients who receive a tip email from Gordy want the service for themselves
children.test: Pilot: send Gordy tip emails and measure how many recipients ask for the service

children.label: Users who receive a Gordy nudge email want the service for themselves
children.test: Pilot: send Gordy nudge emails and measure how many recipients ask for the service

children.label: Some users exposed through the intro-email flow ask Gordy for help staying in touch with a real contact
children.test: MVT: Add a stay-in-touch CTA to intro-flow emails and measure click-through plus creation of at least one tracking request

children.label: We can deliver the nurturing workflow with low ongoing user effort
The product can work without ongoing manual data entry from users
children.test: Prototype test: does the workflow still produce useful outputs when users only connect existing data sources and avoid ongoing manual upkeep?
children.reason: Mapped from old cross-cutting hypothesis obs-f: users want to avoid ongoing data entry.

children.label: A calendar-connected workflow is enough to trigger useful nurturing prompts
children.test: Live prompt experiment on calendar-connected users: do pre/post-meeting signals generate prompts users find useful enough to act on?

children.label: There is a path to willingness to pay and retention
Users can name the KPIs they would use to decide whether Gordy is worth paying for
children.test: Mockup: present candidate KPIs and measure which ones users trust enough to decide whether to keep paying

children.label: The persona will pay for ongoing help if value is proven
children.test: Pricing / prepay / commitment test after a concrete value demo or concierge period

children.label: VC principals may be a concrete initial persona for nurturing
children.test: Mom test: what happens after an intro call when it is too early, and how do VCs try to stay top of mind over time?

children.label: CSMs may be a concrete initial persona for nurturing
children.test: Mom test: do CSMs keep relationship notes or seek relationship intelligence to prevent churn and surface expansion?

children.label: Recruiters may be a concrete initial persona for nurturing
children.test: Adjacent-segment interviews with recruiters about keeping candidates and hiring managers warm

children.label: Richer personal contact context may create better reasons to follow up
External trigger alerts may create useful reasons to reach out
Users want alerts about life or career trigger events for contacts as reasons to reach out
children.test: Mom test: do users actually search for these triggers, set alerts, or follow companies/people to find reasons to reach out?

children.label: Users want alerts about dynamic external data tied to contacts as a zero-effort reason to reach out
children.test: Mom test: do users currently track sports, news, or events about contacts, and do they ever act on it?

children.label: There may be a viable product angle around deeper private contact context
Users want to leverage personal anecdotes on connectors so they can ask more intros
children.test: Mom test: before asking for an intro, do users look for personal anecdotes or small-talk hooks about the connector?

children.label: Users will pay to mine personal intel from their own emails, call transcripts, and WhatsApp to fuel warm relationship actions
children.test: Mockup test: offer mined personal context from private sources and measure willingness to connect data and try it

children.label: Users who have gathered deep personal intel about contacts are willing to share or trade it with others
children.test: Interview: have users shared such intel before, what are they comfortable sharing, and with whom?

children.label: Users show behavioral interest in richer context and deeper briefs
A meaningful minority of users open an extended brief when it is offered
children.test: Add an “extended brief” link to the email and measure click-through to the deeper brief

children.label: Learnings and out-of-scope nurturing branches
Larger-company AEs paying out of pocket for a personal network CRM is weak as an initial path
AEs in companies over 20 will pay out of pocket for a personal network CRM
children.test: Interviews plus willingness-to-pay checks with AEs at larger companies

children.label: AEs want to own their network and move it with them when they change jobs
children.test: Mom test: ask whether reps see their network as a personal asset they want to carry across jobs

children.label: AEs want to claim attribution for deals they influenced through personal relationships
children.test: Mom test: ask for examples where personal relationships influenced deals but attribution was unclear

children.label: There is at least one explosive growth wedge in the introductions and intro-meetings space for small business owners who also do selling
Strategic ask brief / intro-meeting flow is a viable initial wedge for the segment
Small business owners at organizations under 20 who also do selling request introductions or relevant meeting asks often enough to support this wedge
children.test: Analysis on past calls: frequency of asking for intros / relevant asks around meetings
children.reason: Weekly+: 16 users. Monthly: 3. Quarterly or less: 11. Never: 6. Out of ~55 interviewees.

children.label: Users have a real enough pre-meeting intro / strategic-ask need that they want the tool and will connect calendar for it
children.test: Pitch / mockup evidence: over 80% of pitched users wanted the tool and were willing to connect calendar access to get strategic ask briefs
children.reason: 17 intros made out of 20-25 pitched. Over 80% wanted the Ask Brief.

children.label: Current alternatives are weak enough: manual pre-meeting intro / ask preparation is rare, inconsistent, or too effortful
children.test: Interview follow-up plus diary / baseline measurement: quantify how often users do this manually today, what substitutes they use, and how often those substitutes actually lead to asking for intros or strategic asks

children.label: Initial adoption and retention are strong enough for this wedge
Initial adoption works
Users will ask the counterpart for intros in a real meeting when Gordy suggests it
children.test: Post-meeting debrief with active users: after receiving Gordy's strategic ask brief, did the user actually raise the intro ask in the meeting? Measure ask rate and outcome.

children.label: Users retain the product for long enough, and across enough relevant meetings, for growth loops to fire
children.test: Retention analysis on early users: define thresholds and measure how long users stay active, how many relevant meetings they have while active, and whether they remain in the product long enough for growth exposures to occur

children.label: We can generate enough meaningful suggestions for enough meetings to support this wedge
children.test: Live product / concierge evaluation: define coverage and quality thresholds, then measure what share of relevant meetings receive suggestions and what share of those suggestions users judge as meaningful enough to use

children.label: The strategic ask brief flow has at least one working growth loop
At least one Gordy-sent counterpart email loop works
The pre-meeting counterpart notification loop works
Pre-meeting counterpart notification emails are allowed and generated often enough from normal product usage
Users will let Gordy send a pre-meeting counterpart notification email and keep Gordy attribution in the message
children.test: Live experiment plus user interviews: measure whether users allow Gordy to send a pre-meeting counterpart-facing email and whether Gordy attribution remains intact

children.label: Enough eligible meetings result in a pre-meeting counterpart notification email being sent
children.test: Instrument the live flow and measure the share and absolute volume of eligible meetings that produce a pre-meeting counterpart notification email

children.label: Pre-meeting counterpart notification emails get enough attention to drive counterpart user conversion
Counterpart email gets over 30% click rate and 60% notice rate
children.test: Live emails to real attendees — measuring clicks, replies, and notice / recall
children.reason: Only 17.5 (19 out of 109) clicked the hello page. None converted

children.label: Intro Circle CTA gets CTR
children.test: Live emails to real attendees — measuring clicks, replies, and notice / recall

children.label: Hello page CTA gets CTR
children.reason: Only 17.5 (19 out of 109) clicked the hello page. None converted
children.test: Live emails to real attendees — measuring clicks, replies, and notice / recall

children.label: Get Intros CTA gets CTR
children.test: Live emails to real attendees — measuring clicks, replies, and notice / recall

children.label: At least one pre-meeting counterpart CTA converts recipients into activated users
The 'Introduce me to someone' CTA converts recipients into activated users
Some recipients of the pre-meeting counterpart email click an 'Introduce me to someone' CTA
children.test: MVT: Add an 'Introduce me to someone' CTA button to pre-meeting counterpart emails. Measure click rate and downstream intent.

children.label: Recipients who click 'Introduce me to someone' reach a clear Gordy intro-request flow
children.test: Prototype the click destination for the 'Introduce me to someone' CTA and measure whether recipients understand the flow and reach the point where they can request an intro through Gordy.

children.label: Enough recipients who enter the 'Introduce me to someone' flow sign up and activate as users
children.test: Measure the signup and activation rate of recipients who click the 'Introduce me to someone' CTA and enter the Gordy flow.

children.label: The 'Help me stay in touch' CTA converts recipients into activated users
Some recipients of the pre-meeting counterpart email click a 'Help me stay in touch' CTA for a real contact
children.test: MVT: Add a stay-in-touch CTA to pre-meeting counterpart emails. Measure click rate and downstream tracking-request creation.

children.label: Recipients who click 'Help me stay in touch' reach a clear Gordy contact-tracking flow
children.test: Prototype the click destination for the 'Help me stay in touch' CTA and measure whether recipients understand the flow and reach the point where they can set up tracking through Gordy.

children.label: Enough recipients who enter the 'Help me stay in touch' flow sign up and activate as users
children.test: Measure the signup and activation rate of recipients who click the 'Help me stay in touch' CTA and enter the Gordy flow.

children.label: The Gordy-sent post-meeting counterpart follow-up loop works
Gordy-sent post-meeting follow-up to counterparts is generated often enough from normal product usage
Users are willing to let Gordy send post-meeting follow-up to the counterpart
children.test: Live experiment plus user interviews: measure whether users allow post-meeting follow-up to be sent to the counterpart by Gordy

children.label: Enough eligible meetings result in Gordy-sent post-meeting follow-up being sent to the counterpart
children.test: Instrument the live flow and measure the share and absolute volume of eligible meetings that produce Gordy-sent counterpart follow-up

children.label: Gordy-sent post-meeting follow-up to the counterpart produces responses
children.test: Monitor response rates to Gordy-sent post-meeting follow-up sent to the counterpart

children.label: Gordy post meeting follow up to the user leads to counterpart intros
Gordy-initiated follow-up to engaged counterparts gets intro
children.test: When a counterpart views the hello page 3+ times, Gordy sends a follow-up asking for an intro.
children.reason: Sent intro requests about 14 counterparts to 8 users - didn't get one intro. Most of them resented the ask.

children.label: At least one user-sent counterpart email loop with Gordy attribution works
The user-sent post-meeting reminder loop works
User-sent Gordy-drafted post-meeting reminder emails are generated often enough from normal product usage
Users will send a Gordy-drafted post-meeting reminder to the counterpart and keep Gordy attribution in the message
children.test: Live experiment plus user interviews: measure whether users send a Gordy-drafted post-meeting reminder to the counterpart and keep the Gordy mention intact

children.label: Enough eligible meetings result in a user-sent Gordy-drafted post-meeting reminder being sent to the counterpart
children.test: Instrument the live flow and measure the share and absolute volume of eligible meetings that produce a user-sent Gordy-drafted post-meeting reminder

children.label: User-sent Gordy-attributed post-meeting reminders to counterparts produce downstream interest or action
children.test: Live test: offer one-click drafting of post-meeting reminder emails and measure send rate, response rate, and whether recipients ask about or activate Gordy

children.label: Enough wedge users convert from the strategic ask brief flow into the paid nurture product
children.test: Follow-on product test with retained wedge users: measure what share have a real nurture need, want the paid nurture workflow after experiencing the wedge, and are willing to move into that product path

children.label: The amortized cost per converted paid user is low enough to justify this wedge
children.test: Model and then measure the amortized cost per converted paid user, including acquisition, service, and wedge usage costs through paid conversion

children.label: Real intro path / network-truth is a viable wedge for the same segment
The same segment has enough warm-intro demand to justify a real intro path / network-truth wedge
children.test: Analysis on past calls: how often small-business leaders target a specific person through a warm path
children.reason: Q5: 35 described this as a need out of 40 that were asked.

children.label: Users need better help finding or timing warm introductions
Mutual connections are often too nominal to reliably support warm-intro decisions
Small-org leaders describe facing many mutual connections and needing to narrow them to a few realistic intro paths
children.test: Mom test: how often do small-org front-people target a specific person for an intro?
children.reason: Q6: 31 described this as a need out of 33 that were asked.

children.label: Job searchers look for first-degree connections who will actually answer and help with inside submissions
children.test: Interview: when looking for a job, do users search for inside-submission connections they believe will actually respond?

children.label: Many mutual connections increase confidence before a reach-out by signaling acceptance odds
children.test: Interview: when and why do users look at mutual connections before reaching out?

children.label: People check mutual connections to understand the target by seeing who they associate with
children.test: Interview: do users inspect mutuals to understand the target socially before reaching out?

children.label: Askers want to know when a giver makes a new relevant real-world connection so they can ask for an intro
children.test: Mockup test: show new relevant connections and ask whether the user would want such timing alerts

children.label: At least one product form for the network-truth wedge is adoptable
A cc-Gordy warm-path workflow is adoptable
Users will use an auto-composed intro request and keep Gordy attribution in the message
children.test: Data from live experiments plus user interviews on attribution removal

children.label: Users will copy Gordy on outreach so he can manage a warm-path waterfall automatically
children.test: Mockup test: show the cc-Gordy flow and measure understanding plus willingness to use

children.label: A self-map / reality-check product form can create value, be adopted, and expand network truth coverage
Users will install a browser extension and connect their calendar to get a reality check on their network
children.test: MVT: provide a real minimal Chrome extension during interviews. Measure whether users actually complete the install and connect their calendar when given the opportunity.

children.label: Users want a real picture of their own network — active vs dormant connections, response likelihood, and intro balance — not a vanity count
children.test: Demo/waitlist test: show a mockup of a network-health dashboard and measure strong pull / sign-up intent

children.label: Users want to benchmark their network health against peers
children.test: Only test after self-map demand is real: show peer-comparison mockups and ask whether this adds value

children.label: Users with the extension will invite friends to expand network truth coverage
children.test: Mockup test: after showing the value, ask whether they would invite named friends / colleagues

children.label: Users who receive a request to install the extension and connect their calendar will do so
children.test: MVT: send invite emails as if the flow exists and measure install / connect conversion

children.label: An open-the-book shared-map product form could work if the behavior is frequent enough
Askers and givers engage frequently enough in an “open the book” exercise that a shared-map tool would matter
children.test: Mom test: ask both sides about frequency of open-the-book behavior and current friction
children.reason: We couldn't find anyone doing it more than once per quarter.

children.label: Askers see value in seeing which of a helper's connections are real and active before asking
children.test: Mockup test: pretend the shared map exists and ask who they would ask tomorrow

children.label: Givers are willing to install a tool that shares a real map with the asker
children.test: Mockup test: pretend the shared-map flow is real and measure willingness to participate

children.label: We can technically deliver useful real intro paths and warm-path management with current data sources
We can filter 300+ mutual connections down to 3-5 realistic intro paths using active relationship signals
children.test: Prototype / mockup test: produce filtered paths and ask whether users trust the shortlist enough to act

children.label: We can rank connectors by likely responsiveness to both the user and the target
children.test: Prototype / mockup test: show ranking rationale and ask whether users trust it

children.label: We can automate a connector waterfall without double-asking
We can technically implement the waterfall flow
children.test: Feasibility prototype: simulate connector sequencing, fallback, and double-ask prevention

children.label: There is a plausible repeated-use or growth loop once the network-truth workflow works
children.test: Measure whether one solved intro request leads to repeated reuse, sharing, or more warm-path requests over time

children.label: Recipient-side intro outreach itself shows viral / acquisition signal
Recipients respond when Gordy asks for a name-drop or intro confirmation in investor/founder contexts
children.test: Predict who they know and reach out asking for a name-drop / confirmation
children.reason: Investors: 50 > 12 > 3 (25% reply rate). Founders: 58 > 17 > 5 (29% reply rate).

children.label: Recipients also respond to Gordy name-drop requests in AE / BD contexts
children.test: Predict who they know and reach out asking for a name-drop / confirmation
children.reason: AE/Sales: 67 > 26 > 18 (38% reply, 69% confirm). BD: 64 > 23 > 11 (35% reply, 47% confirm).

children.label: Prospects respond to Gordy outbound offers of intros
children.test: Live test: Gordy sends outbound messages offering intros to targeted prospects and we measure human response plus yes rate

children.label: Learnings and eliminated intro-space branches that still matter for interpretation
Broad strategic-reach demand across everyone is too infrequent to be a standalone high-frequency wedge
children.test: Mom tests: how often do users target a specific person for an intro across the broad market?
children.reason: Frequency too low in the broad market, even though a narrower small-business segment still looks promising.

children.label: People do not already practice asking for 2-3 introductions in every meeting
children.test: Mom test: ask for past behavior around routinely asking for 2-3 intros in meetings
children.reason: Only a small minority already do this behavior.

children.label: There may be a business in outbound workflow and extra-intel products
Personalizing outreach to a recipient's specific situation improves response rates materially
children.test: Landscape / research review on response-rate lift from personalization
children.reason: Backlinko large-scale study: 32.7% higher response rate for customized vs generic templates.

children.label: Small-business leaders who do outbound spend meaningful time researching prospects manually
children.test: Analysis of past calls: do people research targets before cold outreach, and how much time does it take?

children.label: Users doing cold outreach have no scalable way to get public personal intel on targets today
children.test: Mom test: how much time does research take, what is frustrating, what have they tried to automate, and would they pay to improve it?

children.label: At least one outbound / extra-intel workflow angle appears promising
Users want to delegate LinkedIn search and inbox/outbox work to an SDR or recruiter
children.test: Mom test: when and why do users want to delegate LinkedIn / email communication, and what triggers the need?

children.label: Users will pay to access personal intel that others have collected about a target they have never met
children.test: Interview + mockup: show the concept and ask whether they want it and whether they are comfortable paying for it

children.label: Users want to take messaging outside of LinkedIn as soon as possible
children.test: Interview: after an initial LinkedIn exchange, when and why do users try to move the conversation to email / phone / other channels?

children.label: LinkedIn inbox is a poor networking tool because messages go unread and signal is buried in noise
children.test: Mom tests across AEs, founders, and VCs about inbox usage and frustration
children.reason: Most people didn't feel this was a primary problem; heavy users already use external tools.
